rationale: The citation is discussed in the theory development, not just listed as background. The citing paper summarizes Hui et al. (2013) in detail (in-store video tracking evidence on unplanned consideration and complementarity) and uses its specific conclusion about limited consideration-to-purchase conversion to motivate its own hypotheses. The new studies are positioned as testing a proposed nudge (complement-based assortment organization) that should convert unplanned consideration into purchases, explicitly building on Hui et al.'s findings.
relation_description: The citing paper builds on Hui et al. (2013) by taking its finding that complementarity raises unplanned consideration but may not convert to purchase, and proposing assortment organization as the mechanism that drives conversion. It extends that line from consideration-stage insight to demonstrated effects on purchases and spending.
